confirmed.summary: Retiring the enforcing terminology gate behind an advisory-only driver is an enforcement downgrade, not preserved coverage
confirmed.evidence: tests/architectural/test_no_legacy_terminology.py:124 hard-fails CI via pytest.fail; the user's CLAUDE.md states 'it enforces the Terminology Canon'. But spec.md FR-005 (line 44) makes the driver 'Advisory/report-only', NFR-002 (line 51) says 'Only the structural validator enforces', and the absence sweep is 'advisory (report-only) in v1'. FR-006 (line 45) + SC-003 (line 72) still 'retire only that portion behind the driver'. NFR-001 (line 50) conflates 'still caught' (detected/reported) with 'still enforced' (blocks merge).
confirmed.recommendation: Do NOT remove or neuter the enforcing terminology assertion in v1 while the sweep is advisory-only. Either keep the enforcing pytest gate until the driver gains a per-record enforcing mode with proven parity, or seed the terminology record as enforcement:enforcing with an enforcing driver path. Add an explicit NFR: an invariant that is enforcing today must remain enforcing after adoption (no enforcing->advisory downgrade). SC-003 parity on fixtures does not scan the live tree, so after removal nothing blocks a newly-introduced forbidden term.

verdict.reasoning:
Finding withstands adversarial scrutiny against the live artifacts. All cited evidence is accurate: (1) /home/jeroennouws/dev/sk-missions/2441/tests/architectural/test_no_legacy_terminology.py:124 is pytest.fail — a merge-blocking (enforcing) gate; (2) CLAUDE.md→AGENTS.md:238 explicitly states it "enforces the Terminology Canon" and that a forbidden-term regression "only fails at CI" via this test, so the repo's own governance treats it as blocking; (3) spec.md FR-005:44 makes the driver "Advisory/report-only"; (4) NFR-002:51 "Only the structural validator enforces"; (5) FR-006:45 + SC-003:72 + edge-case:31 mandate that the terminology test "is removed"/"retired behind the driver" in THIS mission (FR-006 is in scope per C-002:58 "Deliver FR-001..006"); (6) NFR-001:50 says invariants are "still caught (driver+records)" — detection parity, not enforcement parity.

The chain is airtight: today the terminology check blocks merge; FR-006/WP3 (plan.md IC-03, design.md §4:140, §7:182) retire that enforcing test behind an advisory driver; the enforcing driver path (advisory→enforcing flip / per-record enforcement:enforcing) is EXPLICITLY out of scope (C-002:58, out-of-scope:78, design WP6 "optional"; design §6:167 confirms the retirement sweep is report-only). So a newly-introduced forbidden term (ceremony/feature/status-writing) would go from blocking CI to merely reported — a real enforcement downgrade that NFR-001's "still caught" wording conceals by conflating detection with enforcement. This is also an internal contradiction: NFR-001 claims "zero dropped invariants," yet must-block-merge is itself an invariant the gate holds today and would lose.

Refutation attempts all failed: grep found no clause preserving the terminology gate's enforcing status; "keep both test+driver" is ruled out by the explicit removal (spec:31, SC-003); "seed as enforcing" is impossible because no enforcing driver path exists in v1; and it is not stale/already-handled — no driver or registry code exists yet, so the defect lives in the plan as written and would ship the regression if implemented literally. The only imprecision (finding says parity is fixture-based and "does not scan the live tree" — the driver actually does scan scan_roots) is non-load-bearing, because an advisory driver never blocks regardless. Severity high: it downgrades a charter-binding governance gate the repo relies on as merge-blocking; caught pre-implementation makes it cheap to fix but does not reduce impact-if-executed. Recommendation is correct — add an explicit no-enforcing→advisory-downgrade invariant and keep the enforcing terminology gate until an enforcing driver path with proven parity exists.

confirmed.summary: WP3 retires two currently-BLOCKING gates behind an ADVISORY driver — an enforcement regression the spec mislabels as 'zero dropped invariants'
confirmed.evidence: FR-006 (spec.md:45) 'retire only that portion behind the driver' targets test_no_legacy_terminology.py and the path-literal grep; but the driver is 'Advisory/report-only' (FR-005 spec.md:44) and NFR-002 (spec.md:51) says 'the absence sweep is advisory ... Only the structural validator enforces', while 'The enforcement flip (advisory->enforcing)' is Out of Scope (spec.md:78). Both fold targets are enforcing TODAY: test_no_legacy_terminology.py:124 uses pytest.fail, tests/audit/test_no_legacy_path_literals.py:74 uses `assert not violations`. CLAUDE.md documents terminology.py as a relied-upon pre-push blocking gate. NFR-001 (spec.md:50) 'Zero dropped invariants' conflates DETECTION with ENFORCEMENT: an advisory report detects but never fails CI, so a forbidden-term/path regression would stop blocking.
confirmed.recommendation: Do NOT delete the enforcing assertions in this MVP. Gate WP3's 'delete-the-assertion' on ENFORCEMENT parity, not detection parity: keep terminology.py/path_literals.py blocking until a record can be `enforcing` (the deferred flip), or make the driver enforcing for the folded records (which contradicts NFR-002 and must then be re-scoped). As written, WP3 cannot both retire the files and stay advisory without losing a documented blocking gate.

verdict.reasoning: Every load-bearing claim verified against live artifacts and survives adversarial refutation. Both fold targets are BLOCKING today: tests/architectural/test_no_legacy_terminology.py:124 uses pytest.fail; tests/audit/test_no_legacy_path_literals.py:74 uses `assert not violations` in test_no_legacy_path_literals_in_cli_commands (the precise CLI-tree literal-grep half FR-006 folds). FR-006 (spec.md:45) puts their retirement/removal in THIS mission ("retire only that portion behind the driver"; corroborated by design.md:182 WP3 "then retire the 4 files behind the single driver" and edge-case spec.md:31 "a test_no_legacy_* file is removed"). The replacement driver is advisory: FR-005 (spec.md:44) "Advisory/report-only" and — decisively — SC-002 (spec.md:71) "it is advisory (never blocks)." NFR-002 (spec.md:51) "Only the structural validator enforces," and the advisory->enforcing flip is Out of Scope (spec.md:78), deferred to WP6 (design.md:190), with design.md:167 confirming records opt into enforcing only later. So within this mission's scope no folded record can be enforcing. The consequence is an unavoidable enforcement downgrade: after WP3 a forbidden-term or Windows-unsafe-path regression is DETECTED/reported but never fails CI — exactly the invariant AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md:238 relies on ("it enforces the Terminology Canon ... a forbidden-term regression ... only fails at CI"). Yet NFR-001 (spec.md:50) "Zero dropped invariants," SC-003 (spec.md:72) "no coverage lost," and US3 (spec.md:24) frame the fold purely as detection-parity, conflating DETECTION with ENFORCEMENT precisely as the finding states. Every refutation angle collapses: "advisory can still block" is contradicted by SC-002's literal "never blocks"; "retirement is deferred" is contradicted by C-002 delivering FR-001..006 plus FR-006/spec.md:31/design-WP3; "make the record enforcing" is barred by the out-of-scope flip; "it's an accepted tradeoff" fails because the downgrade is never reconciled with the spec's own zero-dropped-invariants/no-coverage-lost success criteria. The contradiction is structural and unresolvable in scope — you cannot both delete the blocking assertion behind an always-advisory driver and preserve enforcement — a DIR-001/DIR-032 conceptual-alignment fork that ironically breaches the mission's own no-dropped-invariants thesis. Severity remains high: if implemented literally it silently converts two documented blocking CI gates (one an explicitly relied-upon pre-push/CI terminology blocker) into advisory-only reports in a governance repo, and the regression is masked by the spec's own success language so downstream reviewers would not notice. Caught pre-implementation and the flip being tracked (WP6) is the only mitigant, insufficient to drop below high given the masking and the documented-blocker reliance.

confirmed.summary: test_no_legacy_agent_profiles_path.py is mischaracterized as a pure directory-existence check and carved out, but it also contains a genuine retired-literal absence sweep that is foldable by FR-006's own criteria
confirmed.evidence: spec.md:14 states the file "asserts directory non-existence" and FR-006 (spec.md:45) carves out "the agent_profiles_path directory-existence check" as non-foldable. Live tests/audit/test_no_legacy_agent_profiles_path.py has TWO tests: test_legacy_agent_profiles_directory_removed (line 45, directory-existence) AND test_no_legacy_agent_profiles_path_literals_in_active_codebase (line 53) which scans src/tests/docs/architecture/research + root files (lines 14-24) for the `agent-profiles` literal — a retired_literal absence sweep structurally identical to what FR-006 folds.
confirmed.recommendation: Either add the literal-sweep half of this file to adoption scope as a retired_literal record, or explicitly justify carving it out (e.g. its pytest.mark.integration pole vs the architectural pole) — do not describe the whole file as "directory-existence". Otherwise NFR-001 ("zero dropped invariants") is unverifiable because the triage misses a live invariant.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against live artifacts and the finding withstands refutation.

FACTUAL CLAIMS ALL CONFIRMED:
- spec.md:14 does characterize `tests/audit/test_no_legacy_agent_profiles_path.py` as asserting "directory non-existence" and lists it among carve-outs ("Only the literal-sweep parts are in adoption scope").
- FR-006 (spec.md:45) explicitly carves out "the `agent_profiles_path` directory-existence check" as do-NOT-fold.
- The live file has exactly two tests: test_legacy_agent_profiles_directory_removed (line 45, genuine directory-existence via LEGACY_PATH.exists()) AND test_no_legacy_agent_profiles_path_literals_in_active_codebase (line 53) which scans ACTIVE_CODEBASE_PATHS (lines 14-24: src, tests, docs, architecture, research + README/CHANGELOG/AGENTS/pyproject) for the `agent-profiles` literal and asserts zero hits. All cited line numbers are exact.
- The literal sweep IS structurally a retired_literal absence sweep (anchor=literal, scan_roots, assert-absent) — the same shape FR-006 folds via path_literals' CLI-grep half (test_no_legacy_path_literals_in_cli_commands, line 61). It is arguably a cleaner fold (plain substring, no comment-skipping). Only the directory-existence test genuinely can't be a literal sweep; the spec wrongly extends "directory-existence" to cover the literal half.

CORROBORATION THE FINDING DID NOT EVEN CLAIM (strengthens it): design.md line 39 characterizes the SAME file as "path-literal absence" and design §7/WP3 lists all four test_no_legacy_* files (this one included) as fold candidates to model as retired_literal records. So spec's reclassification to "directory non-existence" contradicts the mission's own design — a DIR-032 conceptual-alignment divergence. plan.md IC-03 only echoes the spec's mischaracterization; no artifact supplies a valid carve-out justification for the literal half.

REFUTATIONS THAT FAIL: (1) "carve-out=retain, no coverage dropped" is true for the outcome and makes the finding's 'NFR-001 unverifiable' wording somewhat overstated, but does not rescue the carve-out's stated justification, which is factually false for half the file. (2) "integration vs architectural pole" — both this file (line 9) and the folded path_literals (line 54) carry pytest.mark.integration, so pole doesn't distinguish them; the finding pre-empted this escape hatch.

ONLY IMPRECISION: the "NFR-001 is unverifiable" framing is slightly strong since a retained-unchanged file trivially preserves its invariants. But the substantive defect — a foldable retired_literal sweep mislabeled as pure directory-existence, contradicting the design, in a mission whose entire purpose is accurate DIR-041 one-source-of-truth invariant triage — is real, live, non-stale, and unhandled. Severity MEDIUM stands: not HIGH (no actual coverage lost under the conservative scope), not LOW (genuine triage error plus a design/spec conceptual-alignment divergence in the mission's core competency).

confirmed.summary: test_no_legacy_path_literals.py has a third test category (T021/FR-010 hand-rolled home-literal ban) that the plan's two-way "CLI-grep half + behavioral nudge" split does not account for, risking accidental removal
confirmed.evidence: plan.md:38/49 and spec.md:14/25/45 model this file as exactly two parts: the CLI-tree literal-grep half (fold) and the behavioral nudge tests (carve out). Live file has 7 test functions; beyond test_no_legacy_path_literals_in_cli_commands (line 61) and the 3 nudge tests (lines 115-168), there are THREE more: test_no_handrolled_spec_kitty_home_in_global_state_modules (line 246), test_home_literal_guard_is_non_vacuous (line 264), test_bare_spec_kitty_literal_matcher_precision (line 282) — a `.spec-kitty` literal-ban sweep over src/specify_cli+src/kernel (line 184) with _HOME_LITERAL_ALLOWLIST (line 191). These are neither the CLI-grep half nor behavioral nudges. The instruction "keep behavioral tests" (plan.md:49) gives them no home.
confirmed.recommendation: Explicitly enumerate all 7 test functions and their disposition. State that the T021/FR-010 global-state literal-ban tests (issue #2171 regression guard) are retained; if only test_no_legacy_path_literals_in_cli_commands is to be removed, say "remove exactly this one function, keep the file" rather than "adopt the literal-grep half, keep behavioral tests."

verdict.reasoning:
The finding withstands adversarial scrutiny against the live repo. Every factual claim is exact: the live tests/audit/test_no_legacy_path_literals.py has 7 test functions at the cited line numbers (61,115,137,155,246,264,282); three of them (246/264/282) form a .spec-kitty literal-ban sweep over src/specify_cli+src/kernel with _HOME_LITERAL_ALLOWLIST (191) and are genuine #2171 regression guards added by a PRIOR mission (commit a75174917, #2182), not by this mission. They are neither the CLI-tree grep nor behavioral nudges.

The core claim also holds and is in fact understated: grep for handrolled/global-state/T021/FR-010/2171 returns NOTHING in plan.md and spec.md — both model the file as exactly two parts (CLI-grep half to fold + behavioral nudge to carve out). design.md:40 likewise scopes the file only to the CLI tree, and design.md:140 says "retire the 4 files behind the driver," which if anchored on would delete the whole file and take the T021 guards with it. spec.md:14 claims this triage was squad-"verified," which is an overstatement since a category was missed. So it is not stale, not misread, and not already-handled anywhere in the dossier.

Countervailing guardrails exist ("adopt ONLY the literal-grep half," "carve out the rest," delete-the-assertion-not-the-test) and would likely steer a careful implementer to retain the file — this is why it is not high/critical and why no coverage is lost yet. But the risk stays material because: (1) the T021 tests ARE literal-ban/grep tests, so "the literal-grep half" overlaps them (a real over-fold path); (2) the residual "keep behavioral tests" mislabels the T021 static scans; (3) the mission's coverage safety net (NFR-001/SC-003 parity) is scoped to the adopted sweeps only, so it would NOT protect these #2171 guards from silent removal. Mission is pre-implementation, so the ambiguity lands on the implementer. Medium is the right calibration: a real, cheap-to-fix plan-precision gap intersecting a material regression guard with no protecting gate — above low (real over-fold/removal path + false "verified" confidence + design "retire the 4 files"), below high (no coverage lost yet, multiple soft guardrails toward retention, trivial remedy). The recommendation (enumerate all 7 functions; state "remove exactly test_no_legacy_path_literals_in_cli_commands, keep the file") is sound.

confirmed.summary: Adoption target mischaracterized: test_no_legacy_path_literals has a second enforcing static scan (#2171 global-state home guard) the spec's two-part split omits
confirmed.evidence: tests/audit/test_no_legacy_path_literals.py has five tests: the CLI-tree grep (line 61, adopted), three behavioral nudge tests (lines 115/137/155, carved out), AND test_no_handrolled_spec_kitty_home_in_global_state_modules (line 246) -- a SECOND static literal scan over _GLOBAL_STATE_SCAN_ROOTS (line 184) with _HOME_LITERAL_ALLOWLIST (line 191), guarding issue #2171 -- plus its guards (lines 264/282). spec.md:14 and FR-006 (line 45) describe path_literals as only 'the CLI-tree literal-grep half' (adopt) + 'behavioral tests' (carve out); the #2171 global-state static scan is unaccounted for.
confirmed.recommendation: Fix the spec's two-part description to three-part and explicitly classify the #2171 global-state home-literal scan: it is static but different-scope with its own allowlist and enforcing invariant. Carve it out (retain enforcing) or, if folded, prove parity for ITS invariant too. Otherwise the implementer may fold it into the advisory driver and lose the #2171 enforcing coverage, or leave parity ambiguous.

verdict.reasoning: Factual core verified against live artifacts and survives adversarial refutation. tests/audit/test_no_legacy_path_literals.py contains exactly the structure claimed: CLI grep (L61), three behavioral nudges (L115/137/155), and a SECOND static literal scan test_no_handrolled_spec_kitty_home_in_global_state_modules (L246) over _GLOBAL_STATE_SCAN_ROOTS (L184) with _HOME_LITERAL_ALLOWLIST (L191), plus guards (L264/282). That #2171 scan is genuinely distinct: it is ENFORCING (assert not violations at L256, fails the build), scans a different surface (src/specify_cli + src/kernel, not the CLI tree), matches a different literal (bare .spec-kitty), and has its own allowlist — whereas the mission driver is advisory (NFR-002). It is unaccounted-for across the ENTIRE dossier: grep for 2171/handrolled/global-state/FR-010/T021 in spec.md, design.md, plan.md returns zero relevant hits; spec.md:14/25/45 and plan.md:36/38/49 describe path_literals as a two-part split, and design.md:40 actively mischaracterizes the whole file as CLI-tree-scoped literal absence. So the finding is not stale, not misread, and not already-handled — the spec's characterization is genuinely incomplete, and for a mission whose thesis is family-census completeness (NFR-001 zero dropped invariants) that is a legitimate coverage/accuracy gap. Downgraded from medium to low on materiality: the realized coverage-loss risk is well-mitigated. The operative instruction is default-retain ('retire ONLY the named literal-grep half; retain the rest'; plan.md:49 'adopt only the literal-grep half; keep behavioral tests'), so the unnamed #2171 scan is retained by default with enforcing coverage intact. The worst-case fold-in is structurally self-defeating: .spec-kitty is a LIVE segment (confirmed at windows_paths.py:88,90 as Path.home()/'.spec-kitty' plus 4 other allowlisted keystone files), so a status=retired absence sweep would flag those legit uses and fail parity — a retired_literal cannot represent it. Combined with advisory-only + parity-before-removal + delete-assertion-not-test, actual dropped coverage is unlikely. The gap warrants a spec/design correction (three-part characterization; classify the #2171 scan as retained-enforcing) rather than being a coverage-loss trigger, hence low not medium.

confirmed.summary: FR-003 _ratchet_keys promotion forces an import-path migration across 6 live callers that the plan's files-touched list omits; "no behavior change" is misleading
confirmed.evidence: plan.md:29 says promote composite_key "no behavior change to existing callers" and FR-003 (spec.md:42) requires the registry LOADER (src/specify_cli/contracts/registry.py) to depend on it. But live code confirms src/ NEVER imports from tests/ (grep of src/ for `from tests` is empty; enforced by tests/architectural/test_layer_rules.py, test_integration_boundary.py, etc.), so the module MUST physically leave tests/architectural/. Six live importers use `from tests.architectural._ratchet_keys import ...`: test_commit_target_kind_guard.py:51, test_no_write_side_rederivation.py:34, test_gate_read_literal_ban.py:70, test_wp05_write_target_drain.py:41, test_no_worktree_name_guess.py:78 (+ _ratchet_keys.py:15 docstring). The plan's Project Structure (plan.md:41-50) lists NEITHER _ratchet_keys.py NOR any of the 6 importers.
confirmed.recommendation: Add _ratchet_keys.py and its 6 architectural-gate importers to the files-touched inventory; specify the relocation target (an importable shared/src location) and whether importers are rewritten or a re-export shim is left (and if a shim, that it needs a shim-registry entry per test_unregistered_shim_scanner.py). Replace "no behavior change to existing callers" with the accurate "import path changes for 6 callers."

verdict.reasoning:
Core claim withstands refutation. Verified against live artifacts: (1) plan.md:29 verbatim says promote composite_key "no behavior change to existing callers"; (2) FR-003/spec.md:42 + design.md:121/125/176 require the src/ loader (src/specify_cli/contracts/registry.py) to depend on the promoted composite_key; (3) the primitive lives ONLY at tests/architectural/_ratchet_keys.py — `find src` for composite_key/enclosing_qualname returns NONE; (4) src/ runtime never imports tests/ (grep empty of real imports) and tests/ is not shipped in the wheel, so the src/ loader importing it forces the primitive out of tests/architectural/ into an installable location; (5) plan.md:41-50 files-touched block omits _ratchet_keys.py, the promotion target, and every importer. The shim escape hatch (re-export at the old path) does not erase the gap — the plan must still add the new src/ module, convert the old path, and decide rewrite-vs-shim, none of which the inventory specifies. So the substantive recommendation (add the promoted module + target + importer disposition to the inventory) is legitimate and actionable.

Severity drops to low because the finding oversells and carries factual defects: (a) it claims "six live importers" and lists five, but the true module-level importer count is 10 under tests/architectural (+1 in another mission) — imprecise, though it undercounts; (b) it cites test_layer_rules.py and test_integration_boundary.py as enforcing src-must-not-import-tests, but verification shows those tests enforce intra-src layer boundaries (kernel/doctrine/charter/specify_cli) and CORE-vs-INTEGRATION within src/specify_cli respectively — NEITHER enforces the tests boundary; the real basis is packaging reality + grep; (c) "no behavior change is misleading" is an overreach — the phrase is a defensible behavior/semantics claim and a re-export shim keeps the import path identical, so it is not actually misleading; (d) the shim-scanner parenthetical is inaccurate — test_unregistered_shim_scanner.py only fires on modules with __deprecated__ = True, so a plain re-export shim is not caught. The gap is also self-revealing at first test collection and the promotion is already documented in prose across plan.md and design.md; no invariant is violated. It is a real plan-inventory completeness gap worth fixing, but a low-weight one, not a medium boundary risk.

confirmed.summary: test_no_legacy_terminology.py is labeled "a clean literal fold" but carries a companion meta-invariant test that must survive folding
confirmed.evidence: spec.md:14 calls it "a clean literal fold." Live test_no_legacy_terminology.py has 2 tests: the git-grep sweep test_forbidden_term_does_not_appear (line 114) AND test_docs_adr_exemption_is_narrow (line 132), a regression guard (mission 01KW3SBK / 117 ADRs) pinning the docs/adr exemption as NARROW (only docs/adr excluded, not the rest of docs/). This narrowness invariant is not a literal and is not preserved by a retired_literal record's flat `exemptions` list.
confirmed.recommendation: When retiring this file behind the driver, add an explicit test/assertion that the generalized per-record `exemptions` model preserves exemption-narrowness (a docs/adr exemption must not blanket-exempt all of docs/), so NFR-001 does not silently drop the WP06 guard.

verdict.reasoning:
Every load-bearing claim withstands live verification. (1) spec.md:14 reads verbatim "tests/architectural/test_no_legacy_terminology.py (a clean literal fold)" — this is the authoritative adoption-scope statement. (2) AST confirms the file has exactly two live test functions: the parametrized sweep test_forbidden_term_does_not_appear (line 114) AND test_docs_adr_exemption_is_narrow (line 132). (3) The narrowness test is a genuine, currently-enforced regression guard: its docstring cites "WP06 (mission 01KW3SBK): 117 historical ADRs," git commit 64e27fc3b confirms "fix(WP06 cycle3): exempt docs/adr/ … + narrow regression test," and simulation confirms it pins docs/adr/ as excluded while docs/guides, docs/architecture, and top-level docs stay scanned — and that broadening the exemption to "docs/" would wrongly swallow all of docs/. (4) The record model (design §3.1, spec FR-001, plan IC-01) carries only a flat consumers.exemptions data list; the mission's parity proof (spec line 22/26, SC-003, design §4 Slice D / §5.1) is operationalized as a hit-set comparison ("driver flags exactly the terms/paths the old sweep flagged"), which runs against the current tree and structurally cannot detect the loss of a guard that protects against future exemption-broadening.

The gap is real and not already handled: FR-006/plan-WP3 retire terminology.py behind the driver (delete-the-assertion-not-the-test), which removes the file — and the companion narrowness guard with it — while the "clean literal fold" framing actively signals nothing special needs preserving. Tasks are not yet written, so nothing downstream captures it. The strongest refutation — that NFR-001's "zero dropped invariants across the whole family" already requires preserving it — fails, because the requirement's own operationalized proof (hit-set parity) plus the misleading "clean literal fold" label are precisely the two mechanisms that would let this narrowness regression guard drop silently. The finding names the exact invariant the parity proof misses and prescribes the missing explicit assertion.

Severity is correctly rated low: the sweep is advisory in v1, the seeded record's current behavior is in fact preserved by substring matching over "docs/adr/", and what is lost is a meta-guard against a future misconfiguration rather than an active production break. The finding's evidence wording ("not preserved by a flat exemptions list") is slightly imprecise — the current behavior IS preserved by substring semantics; what is not preserved is the regression guard against broadening — but the core concern and the recommendation are precise and correct. real=true, severity=low (unchanged).

dropped.summary: The registered-completeness self-defense gate the design mandates as enforcing-in-v1 was silently dropped from the spec/plan, so the new registry can rot
dropped.why_refuted:
The finding's LITERAL quotes all check out against the live artifacts: design.md:166 does list two enforcing-v1 gates (schema validator + generalized registered-completeness gate); design.md:198 repeats it; tests/architectural/test_unregistered_shim_scanner.py:67-75 is genuinely enforcing (bare `assert`, `architectural` marker, no skip); spec.md FR-002:41 does say "Structural validation is the only enforcing gate in v1"; and spec Out-of-Scope (75-79) does not literally name a registered-completeness gate. So the textual design-vs-spec discrepancy is real. But the finding's HIGH-severity characterization -- "silently dropped self-defense gate ... so the new registry can rot" -- does not withstand scrutiny on four independent grounds, so the finding as stated is not real.

1) The design's OWN executable WP breakdown (§7, lines 175-191) never schedules this gate into any work package. WP1 -- the MVP increment the spec implements -- explicitly reads "loader+validator ... `doctor contracts` validating well-formedness ... No enforcement change, nothing deleted." The registered-completeness gate appears ONLY in the abstract §6 prose (166), the §8 open-decision recommendation (198), and the precedent table (61). The spec/plan faithfully implement exactly the WPs that were scheduled (WP1-3 -> FR-001..006), and plan.md IC-01:28 carries the same "structural validation is the only enforcing gate in v1" line. This is a design-internal prose/schedule inconsistency, not a regression the spec introduced.

2) The gate is structurally impossible to enforce in this MVP, and its enabling precondition IS deferred in Out-of-Scope. test_unregistered_shim_scanner enforces that every `__deprecated__ = True` MODULE is a shim-registry entry -- those modules live in docs/migrations/shim-registry.yaml. The contract registry is a NEW sibling seeded with ONE record; folding shim-registry into it is explicitly Out-of-Scope (spec.md:79) and C-001. A "generalized" completeness gate pointed at the intentionally-partial contract registry would false-red on every existing `__deprecated__` module on day one. It cannot be enforcing until the fold, which is deferred -- so it is transitively deferred via line 79, contrary to the finding's "it just vanished."

3) No protection is removed, so no rot is introduced. Git confirms test_unregistered_shim_scanner.py is UNTOUCHED in this mission (spec/plan phase only; no src/specify_cli/contracts/ or docs/contracts/ code exists yet). The existing self-defense that guards `__deprecated__`-module completeness still enforces for the shim registry. The finding's "the new registry can rot" implies a guard was withdrawn; none was.

4) The MVP never claims the registry owns ALL contracts. Design line 142 mandates "introduce-then-adopt, never big-bang"; the registry deliberately owns only the one seeded + adopted literal contracts, with the other slices retaining their existing machinery (WP4-6 / Out-of-Scope). The finding's failure scenario ("the registry stops owning all contracts") describes an endstate the MVP explicitly disclaims and defers. Additionally, the recommended remedy (mirror test_unregistered_shim_scanner for retired-literals) does not cleanly transplant: `__deprecated__ = True` is a machine-detectable module marker, whereas a `retired_literal` (a term like "ceremony", a path like "~/.kittify") has no analogous marker to enumerate, so the very analogy the finding leans on is the part that does not generalize to v1's kinds.

Residual, legitimate-but-minor point (why adjusted_severity=low, not dismissed entirely): the design called the gate "enforcing in v1" and the spec/plan silently changed that to "structural validation is the only enforcing gate" without a one-line reconciliation/decision note. DIR-003 decision-documentation would prefer an explicit "deferred with the shim-registry fold because the contract registry is intentionally partial during incremental adoption" line in Out-of-Scope or a decision doc. That is a low-severity traceability/documentation nit, not a high structural gap and not a rot vector. Net: the high finding as written is refuted (real=false); the only salvageable residue is a low-severity documentation reconciliation.

dropped.summary: Latent dual-authority: v1 schema permits kind=fallback_name in the new registry while shim-registry already owns fallback_name, with no arbitration rule or cross-registry check
dropped.why_refuted: All of the finding's citations are factually accurate against the live artifacts (spec FR-001 line 40 kind∈{fallback_name,retired_literal}; C-001 line 57 and design.md:197 defer the shim fold; compat/registry.py:113-114 dedups legacy_path only within the shim registry; FR-004 seed + FR-006 adoption are both retired_literal; shim-registry.yaml:21 is `shims: []`; emit_status_transition carved out, guarded by test_no_legacy_status_emit_callers.py with 3 allow-listed callers). But the finding mischaracterizes a deliberate, documented, tracked, zero-v1-instantiation scope decision as a latent ownership defect, and does not withstand adversarial scrutiny for five reasons: (1) The dual-authority is doubly-latent — for split-brain to exist, someone must write a fallback_name record into the contract registry AND the same name must exist in shim-registry, but shim-registry is empty (`shims: []`) and no v1 FR creates any fallback_name record; the v1 registry holds retired_literal records only, so the fallback_name enum value is a dormant vocabulary term with no populating pathway, not a live second authority. (2) The exact sibling-vs-fold / two-artifacts tradeoff is explicitly weighed in design Open Decision #1 (line 197) and scoped out via C-001 (57) and Out-of-Scope (79) as a tracked #2441 follow-up — it is a conscious architectural decision, not an oversight. (3) Ownership IS assigned at design level: §3.2 (117) 'fallback_name is the existing ShimEntry specialization, subsumed unchanged' and §3.4 (125) 'ShimEntry becomes the kind=fallback_name projection' — shim-registry owns fallback_name until the fold, which is a documented arbitration stance proportionate to an advisory v1 whose only enforcing gate is structural well-formedness. (4) The recommendation to narrow the schema to retired_literal directly contradicts an explicit, reasoned design choice (spec.md Edge Cases line 33: v1 deliberately models the two kinds with existing catch-mechanisms and defers signature). (5) The finding's own evidence undercuts it — the emit_status_transition carve-out (repeated across spec lines 14/25/33, plan IC-03 line 38, and git history) is precisely the mechanism keeping the sole concrete fallback_name candidate out of the new registry in v1, so interim arbitration already exists for the only real case. Under the default-to-false standard, this is a re-litigation of settled, documented, near-zero-blast scope, not a surviving defect. If retained at all it is an informational reminder toward the fold follow-up (low), not a medium ownership defect that should reshape the plan.

dropped.summary: Consumer-discovery safeguard (C-003) is bound to deferred machinery, so v1's only records get hand-authored consumer sets -- the 'hand-typed blind' C-003 forbids
dropped.why_refuted:
The finding's factual premises are accurate, but its central impact framing (medium/coverage: "safeguard bound to deferred machinery, records left 'hand-typed blind' that C-003 forbids") does not withstand adversarial refutation against the live artifacts. Three independent refutations collapse the coverage framing:

1. The "parity/review check that substitutes for discovery" the recommendation asks to be defined is ALREADY pervasively specified for the adopted retired-literal sweeps: US3 independent test (spec.md:26), FR-006 "prove parity BEFORE removing that coverage" (:45), NFR-001 "proven by parity before removal" (:50), C-004 red-first parity assertion (:60), SC-003 (:72). The recommendation is largely already implemented.

2. "Hand-typed blind" means authored without ground truth; the retired_literal scan_roots+exemptions are transcribed from an EXISTING PASSING test (test_no_legacy_terminology.py's scan config + _EXCLUDED_PATH_FRAGMENTS) and then parity-proven. FR-004 (:43) and plan IC-01 (plan.md:30) already give the implementer the correct concrete source ("a retired_literal FROM test_no_legacy_terminology.py with its consumer set (scan_roots + exemptions)") — the actionable instruction is not "run test_no_dead_symbols's resolver," so the claimed implementer hazard is mitigated in the spec itself.

3. The reason C-003 exists (a wrong list produces a false-green "verified" hiding a break — "worse than no list") is structurally neutralized in v1: the sweep is advisory/report-only (NFR-002 :51, FR-005 :44, SC-002 :71, US2 :22, plan IC-02) and "only the structural validator enforces." With no green "verified" available in v1, no coverage can be lost even if scan scope were imperfect — so the medium/coverage severity has no coverage to lose.

Confirmed facts (all accurate in the finding): test_no_dead_symbols.py resolves `from <module> import <name>` for (module,name) in __all__ — an import graph over symbols (docstring lines 14-24), incapable of producing dir scan_roots/path-fragment exemptions; design.md:116 assigns that resolver to the signature kind; spec.md:33 + C-002 (:58) defer the signature kind; v1 records are retired_literal (FR-004/FR-006); C-003 (:59) + Assumption (:82) bind discovery to that resolver "never hand-typed blind."

What actually survives is a LOW clarity nit: C-003 and the line-82 Assumption over-generalize "resolver-discovered" to all consumer sets; they should scope resolver-discovery to the signature/fallback_name (dotted-symbol) kinds and state retired_literal consumer sets are transcribe-from-source-sweep + parity-proven. That is a one-line wording tightening, not a medium coverage safeguard gap. The finding accurately quotes the artifacts but misframes a minor internal-consistency wording issue as a coverage hole, overlooking the parity gate and advisory posture that already provide the exact substitute it recommends. Under the adversarial default (real only if it withstands as filed), the medium/coverage finding is refuted; only a downgraded low clarity observation remains.

dropped.summary: FR-003 claims the v1 sweep driver depends on composite_key, but composite_key is Python-AST-only and cannot anchor the terminology sweep's .md/.yaml hits
dropped.why_refuted: Micro-evidence verified and accurate: _ratchet_keys.py:177 ast.parse, :178-179 except SyntaxError->"<module>", :102 tokenize.generate_tokens (composite_key is Python-source-only), and test_no_legacy_terminology.py:37 _EXTENSIONS covers *.md/*.yaml/*.yml (git grep over src/tests/docs). So composite_key genuinely cannot key a fixed literal in a .md/.yaml file. But the finding MISREADS the spec's actual claims and is refuted: (1) FR-005 (spec.md:44) already says "composite_key/literal anchoring" — a disjunction that assigns LITERAL anchoring to the retired_literal path and composite_key to the symbol path; FR-001 (spec.md:40) makes the anchor explicitly "dotted symbol OR fixed literal — never file:line"; design.md:85-86 shows adjacent `symbol:` (signature/fallback_name) vs `literal:` (retired_literal) fields. The spec never asks composite_key to anchor literals, so the recommendation to "correct FR-005" asks for a clarification the artifact already contains. (2) FR-003 (spec.md:42) is a lib-PROMOTION/dependency requirement; "the registry loader + the sweep driver depend on" composite_key is accurate — the loader (FR-002) resolves symbol anchors via composite_key, and the driver reads kind in {retired_literal, fallback_name} (design.md:140) and uses composite_key for the fallback_name path. "Depends on" describes wiring, not "apply composite_key to every record." (3) The finding factually mislabels fallback_name as deferred ("the deferred signature/fallback_name kinds"), but spec edge-case (spec.md:33) and design.md:117/203 make clear only `signature` is deferred; fallback_name is a v1 kind whose anchor IS composite_key — exactly what FR-003 states. The recommendation thus contradicts itself. (4) The claimed harm (implementer forcing an AST tokenizer onto .md/.yaml grep -> "<module>" keys) is speculative and contradicted by FR-001, FR-005, and design.md:85-86, which unmistakably mandate a kind dispatcher (fixed-string grep for retired_literal). The only imprecise text (design.md:163/179 "composite_key anchoring") is not cited by the finding and is self-corrected elsewhere in the same document. Net: the spec.md the implementer follows is self-consistent and correct; the finding proposes a fix the artifacts already implement and rests on a factual misread of which kinds are deferred. Severity remains low but the verdict is not-real.

dropped.summary: New "Contract Registry" (src/specify_cli/contracts/registry.py, ContractRecord) collides in name with the existing RuntimeContractRegistry in mission_loader/registry.py
dropped.why_refuted: Evidence anchors are all factually accurate (mission_loader/registry.py:44/97 defines RuntimeContractRegistry + get_runtime_contract_registry; compat/registry.py:67 defines load_registry; plan.md:27/44 + design.md:125 + spec.md:41 introduce contracts/registry.py with ContractRecord/load_registry/validate_registry). But the CONCLUSION fails adversarial scrutiny on three counts. (1) No actual symbol collision with RuntimeContractRegistry: the new module introduces ContractRecord (a record/dataclass), not ContractRegistry — a word-boundary grep for `ContractRegistry` across src/ and tests/ returns only the RuntimeContractRegistry self-reference. ContractRecord != RuntimeContractRegistry, they live in different packages (contracts vs mission_loader), share no imports, and serve different domains (YAML-loaded governance artifact vs in-memory MissionStepContract overlay). The 'name collision' is vocabulary proximity, which the finding itself hedges. (2) The only genuine name overlap — load_registry/validate_registry matching compat/registry.py — is DELIBERATE, documented design, not drift: design.md:125 and spec.md:41 explicitly state the loader is 'generalized from compat/registry.py' with ShimEntry becoming the kind=fallback_name projection, and design.md:197/spec.md:79 plan to fold shim-registry into the contract registry later. This is the charter's canonical-source/'generalize, don't reinvent' (C-001) mandate. Fully-qualified module namespacing (specify_cli.contracts.registry vs specify_cli.compat.registry) means no import/call-site collision. (3) Per-domain registry.py is a pervasive, accepted convention — already 8 such modules exist (compat, invocation, mission_loader, session_presence/writers, shims, skills, tool_surface, upgrade), and compat/registry.py + mission_loader/registry.py already coexist with 'registry' vocabulary without problems; a ninth under contracts/ is consistent, not a novel risk. The recommended rename to 'ownership-registry' would be worse given the mission is literally contract-ownership-boundary and the artifact is a contracts registry in a contracts/ package. Only kernel of truth: the dossier does not explicitly cross-reference the runtime RuntimeContractRegistry — but that is an informational nicety, not a functional/structural defect. Per the default-to-false instruction, this does not clearly withstand scrutiny; it overstates an intentional, documented generalization plus benign vocabulary overlap as a collision/drift.

dropped.summary: _ratchet_keys promotion is under-sized and absent from the 'files touched' footprint — it forces a src/ relocation plus 11 import-site edits
dropped.why_refuted:
The finding's empirical scaffolding checks out (0 src→tests imports verified; exactly 10 real _ratchet_keys importers verified; the ASCII "files touched" block at plan.md:41-50 does omit the promoted module; FR-003/design do say the loader depends on composite_key), but its headline claim — promotion "forces a src/ relocation plus 11 import-site edits," must "re-green 12 dependent architectural tests," and is "invisible in the footprint" — does not survive refutation.

1) The 11 import-site edits are AVOIDABLE, not forced. Promoting the impl into src/specify_cli/contracts/ and leaving tests/architectural/_ratchet_keys.py as a thin re-export shim keeps all 10 importers byte-identical and re-greens every dependent test with zero per-test work. This is gate-clean and idiomatic (26 architectural test files already import from specify_cli) and is the natural way to honor FR-003's own explicit clause "no behavior change to existing ratchet callers." The finding presents the maximal (full relocation + rewrite all imports) variant as necessary.

2) "Invisible in the footprint" is misleading: the promotion is described three times in the plan prose (Technical Context plan.md:11, Scale/Scope plan.md:18, IC-01 plan.md:29). Only the illustrative file-list block omits it.

3) The claimed 11th touched file, tests/_arch_shard_map.py:191, is a DOCSTRING EXAMPLE naming _ratchet_keys.py as a non-sharded infra module — not an import site; with the shim it needs no edit.

4) Internal contradiction: the recommendation tail says the WP1 retired_literal loader "does not actually need composite_key" and the promotion "can/should move to WP2." That is plausibly correct (the seeded record is a retired_literal with scan_roots/exemptions and no call_sites; only call_sites are composite_key-anchored per design.md:95), but if true it negates the medium finding's own premise that promotion forces a src/ relocation now — the only real consumer (the WP2 sweep driver) is itself a test that can import from tests/.

Residual true kernel warranting at most a LOW advisory: the files-touched block could name the promoted src/ anchoring module and the _ratchet_keys.py shim, and the "defer promotion to its first real consumer (WP2 driver)" sequencing nudge is fair. That is an itemization nit, not a medium sizing gap that recasts a described, shim-able, low-blast change as a hidden highest-blast-radius item. Hence real=false, severity downgraded to low.

dropped.summary: Consumer-set discovery (C-003), the mission's flagged key risk, leans on a resolver that is not a reusable seam
dropped.why_refuted:
Refuted as a scope misread. The finding's factual sub-claims are accurate (verified): test_no_dead_symbols.py is 1632 lines; its resolver is ~17 private underscore functions (all non-test funcs are underscore-prefixed, no __all__/public surface); it has ZERO real importers (every cross-file reference is a comment). It's even weaker than described — _iter_src_python_files is copy-redefined in 6 architectural test files and _resolve_import_from in 2, so "not a reusable seam, extraction is a real effort" holds.

But the load-bearing conclusion — that FR-004/WP1 is mis-sized because seeding needs this unextracted resolver — does not survive, because the WP1 seed does not use the resolver:

1) FR-004 (spec.md:43) seeds a retired_literal from test_no_legacy_terminology.py and explicitly scopes its consumer set to "(scan_roots + exemptions)" — NOT call_sites. Those two values already exist verbatim as Python constants in the target test: _SCAN_ROOTS = ("src","tests","docs") (L36) and _EXCLUDED_PATH_FRAGMENTS (L42-58). Seeding is near-transcription into YAML, not an investigative graph task.

2) The test_no_dead_symbols resolver produces the dotted-symbol import/call graph (call_sites), which serves the signature/fallback_name kinds. spec.md:33 EXPLICITLY defers this: "signature waits until the test_no_dead_symbols import-graph seam is wired (follow-up)" (also C-002). In the FR-001 schema, call_sites? is optional and is not part of the WP1 seed. So the mission already treats resolver-wiring as a separate deferred effort — it is not silently folded into WP1.

3) The v1 mitigation for "a wrong list is worse than no list" is the sweep being ADVISORY (NFR-002; edge-case L29 "The sweep stays advisory in this mission"; design §6 "enforce nothing new"), not resolver-perfect discovery. design.md:160 is a general blast-radius note, not a WP1 obligation; the finding elevates it into one.

The finding conflates the general C-003 import/call-graph discovery constraint (which bites for the DEFERRED signature/call_sites follow-up and for WP4 write-side-ratchet adoption) with the concrete WP1 retired_literal seed that needs only a grep scope + exclusion list already spelled out in the existing test.

Severity lowered to low rather than dismissed: there is a valid latent kernel — reusing/extracting the resolver is a genuine effort (comparable to the _ratchet_keys promotion) — but it applies to the follow-up WPs that actually consume call_sites (deferred signature kind; WP4 externalizing _ADOPTED_MODULES), not to the WP1/FR-004 sizing the finding names. Against the live artifacts, the WP1 seed is much closer to a YAML transcription of existing constants than the "investigative graph task on an unextracted resolver" the finding asserts.

dropped.summary: WP1 is over-bundled and its stated footprint understates realistic LOC
dropped.why_refuted:
The finding does not withstand adversarial scrutiny against the live artifacts. Its peripheral facts are correct (compat/registry.py = 177 L; shim-registry-schema.yaml = 88 L; WP1 lists several deliverables), but every load-bearing claim fails:

1) MISDIRECTED/PREMATURE REMEDIATION. The recommendation is to correct plan.md:41-50 "so tasks.md sizing is not built on an undercount." But the mission's last status event is PlanCompleted — the /tasks step never ran. tasks/ holds only the template README.md and .gitkeep; there is NO tasks.md. Nothing downstream is "built on an undercount," and WP-splitting for sizing is precisely the tasks step's job (not yet performed).

2) NO FOOTPRINT REFERENT. The plan states no LOC figure anywhere (grep-confirmed). plan.md:41-50 is the "Project Structure (files touched)" block — a file list, not a size estimate. The single "(177 L)" appears in Technical Context as a template-origin citation, not a size prediction. The finding manufactures its own ~250-350 L estimate and calls the plan's (nonexistent) number an undercount. There is nothing to "understate."

3) "OVER-BUNDLED" IS REFUTED BY THE DESIGN. design.md §7 WP1 (lines 175-176, the authoritative WP breakdown) explicitly bundles schema + manifest + one-record seed-with-consumer-set + loader/validator + doctor contracts + promote _ratchet_keys.py to a shared lib. plan.md IC-01/WP1 mirrors this one-to-one — it did not over-bundle beyond the design. The finding leans on design.md:169's prose summary (which omits the _ratchet_keys promotion) to argue WP1 exceeds the "conservative increment," but §7 is the formal decomposition and includes the promotion by design. Selective read.

4) THE "TWO CROSS-SUITE PROMOTIONS" DON'T BOTH LIVE IN WP1. The _ratchet_keys promotion is a hard dependency of the loader's content-anchoring, explicitly "no behavior change to existing callers" (plan.md:29) — a behavior-preserving relocation with near-zero blast; splitting it into a prerequisite WP adds coordination without shrinking WP1. The "discovery-seam" is NOT in WP1: WP1's seed is one retired_literal whose consumer set is scan_roots + exemptions (a frozen literal list, design.md:118,150); the heavy import-graph discovery seam (test_no_dead_symbols resolver -> call_sites) is explicitly deferred (design §8 decision 7; WP4+). There is no discovery-seam to carve out of WP1.

5) MISQUOTE + CONFLATION. "safe standalone slice" is attributed to plan.md:18 but actually lives in design.md:175; plan.md:25 says "WP1 (MVP, standalone)." "Standalone/MVP" denotes independent landability at near-zero blast (design.md:169), not low LOC — the finding conflates independence with smallness to construct an "understatement" the plan never made.

Kernel of truth (WP1 is multi-item; the generalized loader will likely exceed 177 L) is real but non-actionable here: the plan claims no size, tasks.md doesn't exist, and the bundling is a deliberate, design-blessed, dependency-driven inclusion. real=false. Severity, if it were real, would be low (cosmetic sizing note), but the finding as written is unsupported by the artifacts.

Relevant artifacts: /home/jeroennouws/dev/sk-missions/2441/kitty-specs/contract-ownership-boundary-01KWYRE5/plan.md (IC-01/WP1 lines 25-30; file-list lines 41-50), .../design.md (line 169 prose vs §7 WP1 lines 175-176), .../status.events.jsonl (last event PlanCompleted), .../tasks/ (README.md + .gitkeep only, no tasks.md); /home/jeroennouws/dev/sk-missions/2441/src/specify_cli/compat/registry.py (177 L), .../kitty-specs/migration-shim-ownership-rules-01KPDYDW/contracts/shim-registry-schema.yaml (88 L), .../tests/architectural/_ratchet_keys.py (229 L).

dropped.summary: New `doctor contracts` lands in doctor.py, a 1118-line module already flagged for a deferred god-module split
dropped.why_refuted:
Every factual claim in the finding checks out against the live repo: src/specify_cli/cli/commands/doctor.py is exactly 1118 lines; CLAUDE.md:491 lists the deferred god-module split (#1623 / FR-012); doctor.py's own module docstring (line 4) confirms "the 16 thin @app.command shells" and there are exactly 16 named @app.command decorators; the shim-registry shell at doctor.py:515 delegates to check_shim_registry (called line 552, defined in compat/doctor.py:65). So the evidence is NOT stale or misread.

However, the finding fails adversarial refutation because its recommendation is already fully prescribed by the very plan/design it reviews — it is not a gap, drift, or risk the plan leaves open. The finding recommends "keep the new logic in an extractable helper (e.g. check_contract_registry) mirroring check_shim_registry, so the subcommand survives the eventual doctor.py split cleanly rather than adding mass to the god-module." The plan and design already do exactly this:
- plan.md:7 defines the whole mission as "Generalize the proven shim-registry chain (schema->manifest->loader->doctor->scanner) into a Contract Registry."
- plan.md:27 and plan.md:44 put the loader/validator (ContractRecord, load_registry, validate_registry) in a NEW dedicated module src/specify_cli/contracts/registry.py, generalized from compat/registry.py — NOT inline in doctor.py.
- design.md:125 and design.md:176 repeat this: logic in contracts/registry.py; "spec-kitty doctor contracts" only "validates well-formedness" (a thin delegating shell).
- design.md:59-60 documents the exact shim pattern being mirrored (loader in compat/registry.py, check in compat/doctor.py, thin CLI shell at cli/commands/doctor.py:515).

Thus the doctor.py change is, by design, a thin shell delegating to contracts/registry.py — structurally identical to the shim-registry shell and equally split-friendly. The finding itself concedes "the addition fits" the existing decomposition and only warns against "ad-hoc inline logic," a scenario the plan explicitly forecloses. The recommendation is therefore redundant with plan.md:27/44 and design.md:125/176 — it advises the plan to do precisely what the plan already commits to. As an actionable finding it adds nothing and does not withstand scrutiny; real=false. If it were real it would sit at low severity, but the correct verdict is already-handled.

Relevant paths: /home/jeroennouws/dev/sk-missions/2441/kitty-specs/contract-ownership-boundary-01KWYRE5/plan.md (lines 7, 27, 44, 46); /home/jeroennouws/dev/sk-missions/2441/kitty-specs/contract-ownership-boundary-01KWYRE5/design.md (lines 59-60, 125, 166, 176); /home/jeroennouws/dev/sk-missions/2441/src/specify_cli/cli/commands/doctor.py (line 4 docstring, 16 @app.command shells, shim-registry shell at 515->552); /home/jeroennouws/dev/sk-missions/2441/src/specify_cli/compat/doctor.py:65 (check_shim_registry); /home/jeroennouws/dev/sk-missions/2441/CLAUDE.md:491 (#1623/FR-012 deferred split).
